full_context: The room index (RI) is a numerical value representing the geometric shape of a room and is used to select the light distribution type of luminaires. In lighting design, the RI value is used to determine whether wide-, medium-, or narrow-distribution luminaires should be selected. In the formula, L is the room length, W is the room width, and h_re is the luminaire mounting (calculation) height.

variable_definitions.name: Room index
variable_definitions.meaning: Numerical indicator characterizing room geometry

variable_definitions.name: Room length
variable_definitions.meaning: Room dimension along the primary lighting direction

variable_definitions.name: Room width
variable_definitions.meaning: Room dimension perpendicular to the lighting direction

variable_definitions.name: Luminaire mounting (calculation) height
variable_definitions.meaning: Vertical distance from the luminaire luminous center to the working plane

potential_questions: Given L=12m, W=8m, h_re=3.5m, find room index RI
task_description: 1. Measure room length \(L\). 2. Measure room width \(W\). 3. Determine luminaire mounting height \(h_{re}\). 4. Compute \(L\times W\). 5. Compute \(L+W\). 6. Compute denominator \(h_{re}\times(L+W)\). 7. Compute \(RI=(L\times W)/(h_{re}\times(L+W))\).

full_context: The maintenance factor K is the ratio of the average illuminance after a period of use to the initial average illuminance when newly installed, and is used as a correction in illuminance calculations. Its value depends on environmental contamination characteristics and luminaire cleaning frequency; maintenance factors for different locations are selected according to Table 7.0.4.

variable_definitions.name: Maintenance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Lumen maintenance factor accounting for luminaire aging and dirt depreciation

variable_definitions.name: Average illuminance after use
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured average illuminance after a certain service period

variable_definitions.name: Initial average illuminance
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial average illuminance when the luminaires are newly installed

potential_questions: A control room has initial average illuminance 500lx, which drops to 400lx after 2 years; find maintenance factor
task_description: 1. Measure average working-plane illuminance after use \(E_{used}\). 2. Obtain initial illuminance \(E_{new}\) at installation. 3. Compute \(K=E_{used}/E_{new}\). 4. Verify reasonableness using Table 7.0.4.

full_context: Load calculation formula for lighting branch circuits, used to determine conductor cross-section and circuit breaker settings. P_z is the installed lighting load, α is the ballast loss factor, and P_s is the socket load. For gas-discharge lamps, take α=0.2; for incandescent lamps, α=0.

variable_definitions.name: Calculated load
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual power carried by the lighting circuit

variable_definitions.name: Installed load
variable_definitions.meaning: Total nameplate power of the lighting fixtures

variable_definitions.name: Ballast loss factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of auxiliary equipment (ballast) power loss

variable_definitions.name: Socket load
variable_definitions.meaning: Power of electrical equipment connected to sockets

potential_questions: A circuit has eight 40W fluorescent lamps (α=0.2) and two 200W socket loads; find calculated load P_js
task_description: 1. Sum lighting installed load \(P_z\). 2. Select \(\alpha\) according to lamp type. 3. Compute \(P_z\times\alpha\). 4. Sum socket load \(P_s\). 5. Compute \(P_{js}=\sum[P_z\times(1+\alpha)+P_s]\).

full_context: Temperature correction formula for conductor ampacity, used to correct ampacity when actual ambient temperature differs from the reference temperature (25°C). k_temp is obtained from Table E based on conductor operating temperature and actual ambient temperature.

variable_definitions.name: Corrected ampacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Current-carrying capability under the actual ambient temperature

variable_definitions.name: Standard ampacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Ampacity at the reference temperature (25°C)

variable_definitions.name: Temperature correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor accounting for temperature effects

potential_questions: A conductor has ampacity 100A at 25°C; ambient temperature is 40°C; find actual ampacity (conductor operating temperature 70°C)
task_description: 1. Determine standard ampacity \(I_{table}\). 2. Measure ambient temperature. 3. Obtain \(k_{temp}\) from Table E. 4. Compute \(I_{corrected}=I_{table}\times k_{temp}\).

full_context: Design formula for total system reserve capacity, used to determine the security reserve capacity of a power system. P_max is the system maximum generation load. Use the higher value (20%) for small systems and the lower value (15%) for large systems.

variable_definitions.name: Reserve capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Spare generation capacity to ensure system security

variable_definitions.name: Maximum generation load
variable_definitions.meaning: Historical maximum load peak of the system

potential_questions: A regional grid has maximum load 1200MW; using large-system criteria, find the reserve capacity range
task_description: 1. Determine historical maximum load \(P_{max}\). 2. Select coefficient (15%–20%) according to system size. 3. Compute \(P_{reserve}=(0.15\text{–}0.20)\times P_{max}\).

full_context: Generator self-excitation criterion, used to check stability when a single generator supplies a long no-load line. If the criterion is not satisfied, limiting measures shall be taken. Q_c is the line charging reactive power, and X_d* is the per-unit synchronous reactance.

variable_definitions.name: Generator capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Generator rated apparent power

variable_definitions.name: Line charging reactive power
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacitive charging reactive power of the no-load line

variable_definitions.name: Per-unit synchronous reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Per-unit value of generator synchronous reactance

potential_questions: Generator S=300MVA, line Q_c=50Mvar, X_d*=1.2; check whether self-excitation may occur
task_description: 1. Determine generator capacity \(W_H\). 2. Determine line charging reactive power \(Q_c\). 3. Obtain per-unit synchronous reactance \(X_d^{*}\). 4. Compute \(Q_c\times X_d^{*}\). 5. Compare \(W_H\) with the product.

full_context: Simplified line voltage loss calculation formula, applicable to purely active loads with uniformly distributed phase loads. ∑P_c·L is the total load moment, C is the voltage-loss calculation coefficient, and S is the conductor cross-sectional area.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage loss rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Percentage voltage drop along the line

variable_definitions.name: Total load moment
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of (load power × line length)

variable_definitions.name: Calculation coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient related to line voltage level and conductor material

variable_definitions.name: Conductor cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Conductor cross-sectional area

potential_questions: A 380V line supplies 20kW over 50m using copper conductor (C=70.8). If ΔU%<5% is required, find minimum conductor cross-section.
task_description: 1. Determine total load power \(\sum P_c\). 2. Measure line length \(L\). 3. Compute load moment \((\sum P_c)\cdot L\). 4. Determine \(C\) for the conductor material. 5. Set allowable \(\Delta U\%\). 6. Solve \(S=((\sum P_c)\cdot L)/(C\times \Delta U\%)\).

full_context: Average illuminance calculation formula using the utilization-factor method, applicable to illuminance calculations for spaces with uniform lighting layout. Φ is luminous flux per lamp, N is the number of luminaires, U is the utilization factor, K is the maintenance factor, and A is the illuminated area.

variable_definitions.name: Average illuminance
variable_definitions.meaning: Average illuminance on the working plane

variable_definitions.name: Luminous flux per lamp
variable_definitions.meaning: Light output of a single luminaire

variable_definitions.name: Number of luminaires
variable_definitions.meaning: Total number of luminaires in the lighting area

variable_definitions.name: Utilization factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Fraction of luminous flux effectively reaching the working plane

variable_definitions.name: Maintenance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Reduction factor accounting for luminaire aging/dirt depreciation

variable_definitions.name: Illuminated area
variable_definitions.meaning: Horizontal projected area of the working plane

potential_questions: A meeting room is 20m×10m and uses 36W fluorescent lamps (Φ=3350lm). Target 300lx, U=0.6, K=0.8. Find number of luminaires.
task_description: 1. Determine target illuminance \(E_c\). 2. Measure illuminated area \(A\). 3. Select luminaire and obtain \(\Phi\). 4. Determine utilization factor \(U\). 5. Select maintenance factor \(K\). 6. Compute \(N=(E_c\times A)/(\Phi\times U\times K)\).

full_context: Power-frequency overvoltage limit criteria for 330kV and above power grids, where U_ph is the highest phase voltage of the system. The line side shall not exceed 1.3 times, and the substation side shall not exceed 1.4 times.

variable_definitions.name: Overvoltage multiple
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of power-frequency voltage to rated maximum phase voltage

variable_definitions.name: Maximum phase voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Highest normal operating phase voltage of the system

potential_questions: For a 500kV system (U_ph=318kV), measured line-side voltage is 380kV; check whether it exceeds the limit
task_description: 1. Obtain system maximum phase voltage \(U_{ph}\). 2. Measure power-frequency overvoltage value \(U_{meas}\). 3. Compute overvoltage multiple \(m=U_{meas}/U_{ph}\). 4. Determine location (line side/substation side). 5. Compare \(m\) with the corresponding limit.

formula: I^{\prime\prime} = I_B^{\prime\prime} + I_D^{\prime\prime} \\ I_B^{\prime\prime} = \frac{U}{\sqrt{3} \sqrt{R_{\Sigma}^2 + X_{\Sigma}^2}} \\ I_D^{\prime\prime} = 4.3I_{nd} \times 10^{-3} = 8.2P_{nd} \times 10^{-3}
full_context: In short-circuit current calculation of low-voltage auxiliary power systems, the initial value of the periodic component of three-phase short-circuit current consists of the source-supplied component of the auxiliary power supply and the motor feedback component. The formula is applicable to short-circuit current calculations for main switchboards and important distribution boards. Calculation shall consider resistance and the feedback current of asynchronous motors above 20kW; circuit voltage is taken as 400V.

variable_definitions.name: Initial RMS value of the periodic component of three-phase short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value of the periodic component at short-circuit inception

variable_definitions.name: Source-supplied short-circuit current component
variable_definitions.meaning: Periodic component of short-circuit current supplied by the power source/system

variable_definitions.name: Motor feedback current component
variable_definitions.meaning: Periodic component of short-circuit current contributed by induction motor backfeed

variable_definitions.name: Auxiliary power source line voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage on the transformer low-voltage side

variable_definitions.name: Total per-phase loop resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Total resistance of the short-circuit loop per phase

variable_definitions.name: Total per-phase loop reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Total reactance of the short-circuit loop per phase

variable_definitions.name: Sum of rated motor currents considered for feedback
variable_definitions.meaning: Total rated current of motors participating in feedback

variable_definitions.name: Sum of rated motor capacities considered for feedback
variable_definitions.meaning: Total rated power of motors participating in feedback

potential_questions: Given U=400V, RΣ=15mΩ, XΣ=20mΩ, I_nd=500A, find I''
task_description: 1. Compute source component \(I_B^{\prime\prime}=400/(\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{15^2+20^2})\). 2. Compute motor component \(I_D^{\prime\prime}=4.3\times I_{nd}\times 10^{-3}=4.3\times 500\times 10^{-3}\). 3. Sum to get \(I^{\prime\prime}=I_B^{\prime\prime}+I_D^{\prime\prime}\).

full_context: When the plant common power supply and unit auxiliary power supply are supplied separately, the maximum auxiliary power load is calculated using the comprehensive coefficient method. Values of K_z and K_g are determined by plant scale: for large plants take 0.76; for medium plants take 0.76–0.78. ΣP_z and ΣP_g are the sums of rated power of unit auxiliary loads and common loads, respectively.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum auxiliary power load
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum electrical capacity required for plant operation

variable_definitions.name: Comprehensive coefficient for unit auxiliary loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of simultaneity factor and load factor for unit auxiliary loads

variable_definitions.name: Sum of unit auxiliary loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of rated power of all unit auxiliary equipment

variable_definitions.name: Comprehensive coefficient for common loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of simultaneity factor and load factor for common loads

variable_definitions.name: Sum of common loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of rated power of all plant common electrical equipment

potential_questions: Given a large hydropower plant with ΣPz=1200kW and ΣPg=800kW, find Sjs
task_description: 1. Determine plant scale and select \(K_z\) and \(K_g\). 2. Compute \(K_z\times\sum P_z\). 3. Compute \(K_g\times\sum P_g\). 4. Sum to get \(S_{js}=K_z\sum P_z+K_g\sum P_g\).

full_context: For a hydropower plant with an effectively grounded system, the grounding resistance shall satisfy this formula, where I is the maximum RMS ground-fault current entering the grounding device under the maximum operating mode in the design planning year. Grounding resistance shall consider seasonal variations; in high soil-resistivity areas it may be relaxed to a ground potential rise of 5000V.

variable_definitions.name: Grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Resistance of the grounding device to earth

variable_definitions.name: Maximum earth-injected current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value of the current flowing into earth through the grounding device during a ground fault

potential_questions: Given I=25kA, find the maximum allowable R
task_description: 1. Obtain maximum earth-injected current \(I\). 2. Compute \(2000/I\). 3. Obtain allowable \(R\) value.

full_context: For an effectively grounded system under a ground fault, the allowable values of touch potential difference E_j and step potential difference E_k are calculated by the formula. ρ_s is the surface-layer resistivity, C_s is the surface-layer derating factor, and t_s is the fault duration (the same as the thermal stability verification time).

variable_definitions.name: Allowable touch potential difference
variable_definitions.meaning: Limit of touch voltage that the human body can withstand

variable_definitions.name: Allowable step potential difference
variable_definitions.meaning: Limit of step voltage that the human body can withstand

variable_definitions.name: Surface-layer soil resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Electrical resistivity characteristic of surface soil layer

variable_definitions.name: Surface-layer derating factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Attenuation factor of the surface layer for potential distribution

variable_definitions.name: Fault duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration of ground-fault current

potential_questions: Given ρs=200Ω·m, Cs=0.7, ts=0.5s, find Ej and Ek
task_description: 1. Compute numerator \(174+0.17\rho_s C_s\). 2. Compute denominator \(\sqrt{t_s}\). 3. Divide to obtain allowable \(E_j\) and \(E_k\).

full_context: Thermal stability verification formula for the minimum cross-sectional area of grounding conductors. I_jd is the maximum ground-fault current through the conductor (including DC component), t_d is the equivalent fault duration, and C is the material thermal stability coefficient (copper 249–268, steel 70).

variable_definitions.name: Minimum grounding conductor cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum cross-section satisfying thermal stability

variable_definitions.name: Maximum ground-fault current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS ground-fault current including DC component

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent fault duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration of ground-fault current

variable_definitions.name: Material thermal stability coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Thermal stability parameter of conductor material

potential_questions: For a copper conductor with Ijd=30kA, td=0.6s, C=250, find Sjd
task_description: 1. Compute \(I_{jd}\sqrt{t_d}\). 2. Divide by \(C\). 3. Obtain minimum cross-section \(S_{jd}\).

full_context: In power system planning and design, calculation of the maximum natural reactive power load of the grid is the basis for configuring reactive power compensation equipment. Formula (1) defines the relationship between maximum natural reactive load and active load, considering grid structure, load characteristics, and other factors. It is used for reactive source–load balance configuration during peak or valley periods to ensure voltage quality.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum natural reactive power load of the grid
variable_definitions.meaning: Total reactive load of the whole grid before compensation, including user loads, plant auxiliary loads, and network losses

variable_definitions.name: Maximum active power load of the grid
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum value of the algebraic sum of generator output and imported active power in the grid

variable_definitions.name: Maximum natural reactive load coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient related to grid structure and load composition; can be measured or estimated from Table 1

potential_questions: Given a grid with P_D=5000kW and K=1.2, calculate Q_D
task_description: 1. Obtain maximum active load \(P_D\). 2. Determine reactive load coefficient \(K\). 3. Compute \(Q_D=K\times P_D\). 4. Output \(Q_D\).

full_context: To meet voltage stability requirements, the total capacitive reactive power compensation capacity for 220kV and below grids should be calculated. Formula (2) is based on the maximum natural reactive load minus the inherent reactive power supply of the system, ensuring reactive power balance after compensation. It is applicable to compensation equipment capacity planning at peak load of the grid.

variable_definitions.name: Total capacitive reactive compensation capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity of capacitive reactive power compensation equipment to be installed

variable_definitions.name: Maximum natural reactive power load
variable_definitions.meaning: As defined by Q_D = K P_D

variable_definitions.name: Reactive power from local generators
variable_definitions.meaning: Adjustable reactive power output of local generators

variable_definitions.name: Reactive power imported from main/neighboring grids
variable_definitions.meaning: Reactive power imported from external networks

variable_definitions.name: Line charging reactive power
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacitive charging reactive power of lines and cables

potential_questions: Given Q_D=8000kvar, Q_G=2000kvar, Q_R=1000kvar, Q_L=500kvar, calculate Q_C
task_description: 1. Obtain \(Q_D\), \(Q_G\), \(Q_R\), \(Q_L\). 2. Compute \(1.15\times Q_D\). 3. Subtract \(Q_G\), \(Q_R\), and \(Q_L\). 4. Output \(Q_C\).

full_context: The reactive power compensation degree indicates the level of reactive compensation of the grid and reflects compensation capacity per unit active load. Formula (3) is used to evaluate adequacy of compensation, guiding optimization of reactive equipment configuration and avoiding over-compensation or under-compensation.

variable_definitions.name: Reactive power compensation degree
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of capacitive reactive compensation capacity to active load

variable_definitions.name: Capacitive reactive compensation capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: As defined by Q_C = 1.15Q_D - Q_G - Q_R - Q_L

variable_definitions.name: Maximum active load
variable_definitions.meaning: As defined in Q_D = K P_D

potential_questions: Given Q_C=6000kvar and P_D=5000kW, calculate W_B
task_description: 1. Obtain \(Q_C\) and \(P_D\). 2. Compute \(W_B=Q_C/P_D\). 3. Output \(W_B\).

full_context: Accurate calculation of the natural reactive power load coefficient K needs to consider voltage influence. Formula (A) calculates K based on measured data, where the voltage correction term reflects the impact of operating voltage deviation from the rated value. It is used for more accurate prediction of reactive load in grid planning.

variable_definitions.name: Natural reactive power load coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient characterizing the relationship between reactive and active loads

variable_definitions.name: Generator reactive power output
variable_definitions.meaning: Same parameter meaning as in Q_C = 1.15Q_D − Q_G − Q_R − Q_L (reactive output from local generators)

variable_definitions.name: Imported reactive power
variable_definitions.meaning: Same parameter meaning as in Q_C = 1.15Q_D − Q_G − Q_R − Q_L (reactive power imported from main/neighboring grids)

variable_definitions.name: Capacitive compensation capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Same parameter meaning as in Q_C = 1.15Q_D − Q_G − Q_R − Q_L (installed capacitive compensation)

variable_definitions.name: Line charging reactive power
variable_definitions.meaning: Same parameter meaning as in Q_C = 1.15Q_D − Q_G − Q_R − Q_L (line/cable charging reactive power)

variable_definitions.name: Generator active power output
variable_definitions.meaning: Active power output of local generators

variable_definitions.name: Imported active power
variable_definitions.meaning: Active power imported from the main/neighboring grids

variable_definitions.name: System rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal voltage of the power grid

variable_definitions.name: Measured voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured operating voltage of the power grid at the point of measurement

variable_definitions.name: Voltage exponent for active load
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient describing the influence of voltage variation on active load (typical value 0.5)

variable_definitions.name: Voltage exponent for reactive load
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient describing the influence of voltage variation on reactive load (typical value 2.5)

potential_questions: Given Q_G=3000kvar, Q_R=1500kvar, Q_C=2000kvar, Q_L=500kvar, P_G=4000kW, P_R=1000kW, U_N=220kV, U=215kV, α=0.5, β=2.5, calculate K
task_description: 1. Sum \(Q_G+Q_R+Q_C+Q_L\) to obtain \(Q_{\text{sum}}\). 2. Sum \(P_G+P_R\) to obtain \(P_{\text{sum}}\). 3. Compute voltage correction base \(1.05U_N/U\). 4. Compute exponent \((\beta-\alpha)\). 5. Raise the voltage correction term: \((1.05U_N/U)^{(\beta-\alpha)}\). 6. Compute \(K=(Q_{\text{sum}}/P_{\text{sum}})\times(1.05U_N/U)^{(\beta-\alpha)}\). 7. Output \(K\).

full_context: Calculation of the reference value of stator winding insulation resistance for hydro-generators is key for insulation performance assessment. The formula defines the minimum allowable insulation resistance at 100°C, used to judge whether the winding is dry and clean and to ensure safe equipment operation.

variable_definitions.name: Reference insulation resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum insulation resistance value at 100°C

variable_definitions.name: Rated line voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Design rated voltage of the generator

variable_definitions.name: Rated capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal apparent power of the generator

potential_questions: Given U_N=13800V and S_N=300MVA, calculate R
task_description: 1. Obtain \(U_N\) and \(S_N\). 2. Compute denominator \(1000+0.01\times S_N\). 3. Compute \(R=U_N/(1000+0.01\times S_N)\). 4. Output \(R\).

full_context: Insulation resistance at actual temperature should be converted using the temperature correction formula. The formula converts the measured resistance at room temperature t°C (R_t) to the reference value R for comparison with the minimum requirement. The correction factor 1.6 is determined based on insulation material characteristics.

variable_definitions.name: Measured insulation resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Insulation resistance measured at room temperature t°C

variable_definitions.name: Reference insulation resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: As defined by R = U_N/(1000 + 0.01 S_N)

variable_definitions.name: Room temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature at the time of measurement

potential_questions: Given R=500MΩ and t=30°C, calculate R_t
task_description: 1. Obtain reference resistance \(R\) and room temperature \(t\). 2. Compute temperature difference \((100-t)\). 3. Compute exponent \((100-t)/10\). 4. Compute \(1.6^{(100-t)/10}\). 5. Compute \(R_t=R\times 1.6^{(100-t)/10}\). 6. Output \(R_t\).

full_context: The rating of an arc-suppression coil should be determined based on the grid development plan for the target year and shall be calculated as: W = 1.35 I_C U_n / √3. Where W is the arc-suppression coil capacity (kVA), I_C is the capacitive earth-fault current (A), and U_n is the nominal system voltage (kV). This formula is used for sizing arc-suppression coils in resonant grounded neutral systems to effectively compensate capacitive current during single-phase-to-ground faults.

variable_definitions.name: Arc-suppression coil capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated apparent power capacity of the arc-suppression coil

variable_definitions.name: Capacitive earth-fault current
variable_definitions.meaning: Single-phase-to-ground capacitive current when the system neutral is ungrounded

variable_definitions.name: Nominal system voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal line voltage of the power system

potential_questions: Given I_C=50A and U_n=110kV, calculate arc-suppression coil capacity
task_description: 1. Obtain system capacitive current \(I_C\). 2. Determine nominal system voltage \(U_n\). 3. Compute \(U_n/\sqrt{3}\). 4. Compute \(1.35\times I_C\). 5. Multiply results from steps 3 and 4 to obtain \(W\). 6. Output arc-suppression coil capacity \(W\).

full_context: The value of the high resistance connected to a generator neutral affects the healthy-phase temporary overvoltage. If the healthy-phase temporary overvoltage is to be limited below 2.6 p.u., the connected resistance value should be calculated by: R = 1/(2π f C). This formula is used to determine the resistance value in a generator neutral high-resistance grounding system to limit overvoltage during single-phase-to-ground faults.

variable_definitions.name: Neutral grounding resistor
variable_definitions.meaning: Resistance connected at the generator neutral point

variable_definitions.name: Operating frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated system frequency of the generator

variable_definitions.name: System-to-ground capacitance
variable_definitions.meaning: Total three-phase-to-ground capacitance of the generator voltage system

potential_questions: Given f=50Hz and C=0.5μF, calculate neutral resistor R
task_description: 1. Determine system frequency \(f\). 2. Measure total three-phase-to-ground capacitance \(C\). 3. Compute \(2\pi f\). 4. Compute \(2\pi fC\). 5. Take the reciprocal to get \(R=1/(2\pi fC)\). 6. Output resistance value \(R\).

full_context: When the transformer neutral is grounded through one-third of the transformer zero-sequence reactance, under a single-phase-to-ground fault, the transformer neutral zero-sequence voltage can be calculated as: U_0 = 0.5k/(k+2) · U_xg. This formula is used to calculate neutral-point voltage during a single-phase fault in a small-reactance grounded transformer neutral system, where k = X_0/X_1 ≤ 3.

variable_definitions.name: Neutral-point zero-sequence voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Transformer neutral-to-ground voltage under a single-phase-to-ground fault

variable_definitions.name: Zero-sequence to positive-sequence reactance ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of system zero-sequence reactance to positive-sequence reactance

variable_definitions.name: System maximum operating phase voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Phase-voltage component corresponding to the system maximum line voltage

potential_questions: Given k=3 and U_xg=146kV, calculate neutral voltage U_0
task_description: 1. Obtain system parameter \(k=X_0/X_1\). 2. Determine system maximum phase voltage \(U_{xg}\). 3. Compute numerator \(0.5k\). 4. Compute denominator \(k+2\). 5. Compute \(U_0=\frac{0.5k}{k+2}U_{xg}\). 6. Output neutral-point voltage \(U_0\).

full_context: Power-frequency overvoltage at the receiving end of a plant transmission line under no-load operation: U_g = E_d / [cosλ − (X_s/Z)sinλ], where λ = ωl/ν; E_d is the equivalent sending-end system EMF (kV), X_s is the equivalent sending-end system reactance (Ω), Z is the surge impedance of the line (Ω), l is the line length (km), and ν is the wave propagation velocity (km/s). This formula is used to predict power-frequency overvoltage at the end of an unloaded line.

variable_definitions.name: Receiving-end power-frequency overvoltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage rise at the end of an unloaded line

variable_definitions.name: Sending-end equivalent EMF
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent source EMF of the sending-end system

variable_definitions.name: Electrical length parameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Line electrical length parameter

variable_definitions.name: Sending-end system reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent reactance of the sending-end system

variable_definitions.name: Line surge impedance
variable_definitions.meaning: Characteristic (surge) impedance of the transmission line

potential_questions: Given E_d=550kV, λ=π/4, X_s=150Ω, Z=300Ω, calculate U_g
task_description: 1. Determine sending-end EMF \(E_d\). 2. Determine line parameter \(\lambda\). 3. Compute \(\cos\lambda\) and \(\sin\lambda\). 4. Compute \(X_s/Z\). 5. Compute denominator \(\cos\lambda-(X_s/Z)\sin\lambda\). 6. Compute \(U_g=E_d/\text{(denominator)}\). 7. Output overvoltage value \(U_g\).

full_context: The probability that a lightning current amplitude exceeds I can be calculated by: lgP = −I/88. This formula represents the probability distribution of lightning current amplitude and is used to compute the probability P that the lightning peak current exceeds a given value I (kA). It is applicable to most regions of China except the northwest.

variable_definitions.name: Probability of lightning current amplitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Probability that lightning peak current exceeds the specified amplitude

variable_definitions.name: Lightning current amplitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Peak current of a lightning discharge

potential_questions: Calculate the probability that lightning current exceeds 100kA
task_description: 1. Input lightning current amplitude \(I\) (kA). 2. Compute \(I/88\). 3. Apply the negative sign to obtain \(\lg P=-I/88\). 4. Take antilogarithm to obtain \(P\). 5. Output probability value \(P\).

full_context: The air clearance between an independent lightning rod and live parts of switchgear shall satisfy: S_k ≥ 0.2R_ch + 0.1h. This formula is used to determine the safety clearance of an independent lightning rod to prevent backflashover accidents, where R_ch is the impulse grounding resistance (Ω) and h is the height of the lightning rod check point (m).

variable_definitions.name: Air clearance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum air gap between the lightning rod and energized parts

variable_definitions.name: Impulse grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Grounding resistance value under lightning current impulse

variable_definitions.name: Lightning rod height
variable_definitions.meaning: Height of the lightning rod at the check point location

potential_questions: Given R_ch=10Ω and h=20m, calculate minimum air clearance S_k
task_description: 1. Measure impulse grounding resistance \(R_{ch}\). 2. Determine lightning rod height \(h\). 3. Compute \(0.2\times R_{ch}\). 4. Compute \(0.1\times h\). 5. Sum to obtain minimum clearance \(S_k\). 6. Verify actual clearance \(\ge S_k\).

full_context: Determination of the number of insulator units per string based on power-frequency leakage (creepage) distance: m ≥ (λU_m)/(K_e L_0). This formula is used for selecting the number of insulator units for high-voltage lines, where λ is the specific creepage distance (mm/kV), U_m is the highest system operating voltage (kV), K_e is the effective coefficient of insulator creepage distance, and L_0 is the geometric creepage distance of a single insulator unit (mm).

variable_definitions.name: Number of insulator units
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of units in each insulator string

variable_definitions.name: Specific creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Required creepage distance per unit voltage

variable_definitions.name: Highest system voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Highest operating line voltage of the system

variable_definitions.name: Effective coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Effective utilization coefficient of insulator creepage distance

variable_definitions.name: Creepage distance per unit
variable_definitions.meaning: Geometric creepage distance of a single insulator unit

potential_questions: Given a 220kV line with λ=25mm/kV, U_m=252kV, K_e=1, L_0=450mm, calculate m
task_description: 1. Determine specific creepage distance \(\lambda\). 2. Obtain highest system voltage \(U_m\). 3. Determine effective coefficient \(K_e\). 4. Obtain creepage distance per unit \(L_0\). 5. Compute \(\lambda U_m\). 6. Compute \(K_e L_0\). 7. Divide to get minimum \(m\). 8. Round up \(m\) to an integer.
